title: Rotang rudentum
taxonomy: Rotang rudentum belongs to the family Apocynaceae, and the genus Rotang, also known as the climbing fig. This plant is native to tropical Asia, specifically in countries such as India, Malaysia, and the Philippines. The classification of Rotang rudentum is further divided into the species level, with it being identified as a species of flowering plant. The plant's botanical classification also includes its kingdom, which is Plantae, and its order, Gentianales. Understanding the classification of Rotang rudentum is essential for its identification and conservation.
uses: Rotang rudentum, also known as the Indian staff vine, has been utilized in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant's roots are used to treat fever, diarrhea, and other gastrointestinal issues. In Ayurvedic medicine, it is believed to have anti-inflammatory properties and is used to treat conditions such as arthritis. Additionally, the plant is thought to have antimicrobial properties, which may help in wound healing. However, more research is needed to confirm the plant's medicinal uses and potential side effects.

parts: Rotang rudentum is commonly known as the "Roxburgh Fig" or "Indian Fig". The medicinal parts of Rotang rudentum are not well-documented in mainstream literature. side_effects: Rotang rudentum is known to be associated with several side effects, including gastrointestinal issues, such as nausea and vomiting, due to its emetic properties. It may also cause abdominal pain, diarrhea, and in severe cases, dehydration. Additionally, Rotang rudentum can lead to cardiovascular problems, including arrhythmias and decreased blood pressure, which may be life-threatening in some cases. The plant's side effects can be severe and potentially fatal, particularly when used in excess or without proper medical supervision. Overall, its use requires caution and careful consideration.
